title: Successes and challenges of using Semidefinite Programming for the study of Spin Chain Hamiltonians
authors: David Berenstein, P. N. Thomas Lloyd
abstract: We study semidefinite programming (SDP) methods to analyze spin chain Hamiltonians. We examine the ground state energy, the first excited charged states and ground state correlators in two simple models: the Ising model in a transverse magnetic field and the closely related 3-state Potts model. Our goal is to understand precisely what the SDP program is doing and when it works well, why it does so. We focus on the following novel ingredients: using charge constraints to obtain excited states and to see if additional constraints from integrable models are effective at improving the method.   At criticality we also explore to what extent we can use approximate Virasoro correlators to extract conformal data: the central charge and some critical exponents of charged states. We also use these to identify the location of the phase transition. In the special case where the system is made of free fermions we prove that the SDP finds the exact energy of the ground state and produces the correct two point functions of the fermions. Away from free fermion theories, the SDP gets progressively worse at estimating data beyond the value of the ground state energy (like correlation functions), although it qualitatively matches these. In order to be effective, the SDP seems to run into scaling issues where the amount of input needed scales poorly with the lattice volume.
summary: This paper analyzes the utility of Semidefinite Programming (SDP) for studying quantum spin chains, testing its ability to calculate ground state energies and correlation functions for models like the Ising and Potts chains. While SDP provides exact results for free fermion systems, its performance degrades for interacting models, highlighting the need for incorporating deeper physical constraints to make the method scalable and accurate.
main_problem: The paper investigates the effectiveness and limitations of using Semidefinite Programming (SDP) methods to analyze spin chain Hamiltonians, aiming to determine when and why the method successfully extracts physical properties.
main_result: SDP successfully finds the exact ground state energy and two-point functions for free fermion systems, but its ability to estimate higher-order correlators degrades significantly for interacting models, and the method suffers from poor scaling with lattice volume.
method: The core method is formulating the problem as a convex optimization problem (minimizing the expectation value of the Hamiltonian) subject to positivity constraints on the density matrix, which is solved via SDP.
model_or_system: The study focuses on spin chain Hamiltonians, specifically applying the technique to the transverse-field Ising model and the 3-state Potts model, and analyzing the behavior in the free fermion limit.
key_observables: Ground state energy ($E_0/N$), first excited state energy ($E_1/N$), ground state correlators, and conformal data (central charge, critical exponents) at criticality.
important_parameters: The coupling constant ($\mu$), lattice volume ($N$), and the specific symmetry constraints (charge, Virasoro generators) used in the SDP formulation.
main_assumptions: The method relies on the ability to express the problem as a convex optimization problem, and its success is strongly tied to the system being exactly solvable (free fermions).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the convergence of SDP results for $E_0/N$ and $E_1/N$ for the Ising and Potts models, and show the construction and consistency checks using lattice Virasoro generators.
paper_structure: The paper systematically introduces the SDP formulation, applies it to specific models (Ising, Potts), and then explores advanced diagnostics by incorporating symmetry constraints (charge, Virasoro) and analyzing the free fermion limit.
why_interesting: The rigorous application of convex optimization (SDP) to quantum many-body systems provides a powerful, albeit computationally demanding, theoretical tool for bounding and calculating quantum observables, especially useful for understanding critical phenomena.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
